text: Welcome to Fragmented, a software developer podcast where we talk about building good software and becoming better developers.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: My name's Don Felker.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And I'm Kaushik Gopal.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Welcome to the show.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Before we get started here today, I wanted to tell you about the relaunch of AndroidJobs.io.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Android Jobs is a job board that I launched many years ago, and I just recently relaunched it with a brand new design and on a brand new platform.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Eventually they'll be paid, but right now they're all free at the time of this recording.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So please go to androidjobs.io and you can post a job for free.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Or if you're looking to see what other jobs are available, you can also go there and plug your email address in.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And we'll start emailing you here very soon, letting you know weekly about the new jobs that are available on androidjobs.io.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Again, check it out at androidjobs.io.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Hey folks, welcome back to today's show.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Thanks again for joining me.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If you're not familiar with who I am, my name is Don Felker.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I am one of the co-hosts here on the show on the Fragmented Podcast, where we talk about all different types of software development topics.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And today we're gonna talk about something that's been asked about quite a bit, and that's how to pick what technology to learn next.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: there's a wide array of things that you can learn out there in the world.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And especially in technology, it's really something that can give you quite a bit of analysis paralysis when you're thinking about it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And as I sit here talking about this, I look over and I see a book that I'm currently reading and it's called Great Leads.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And the subtitle is The Six Easiest Ways to Start Any Sales Message.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Now you may be wondering, what does this have to do with the software podcast?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Why am I talking about a copywriting book that I'm reading?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Simply because this is something different than I'm learning.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's copywriting.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And you might be wondering again, why does copywriting even have anything to do with software?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If you're like me, you've probably have had multiple side projects and you might've even shipped some of them.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I have shipped quite a few.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I have run a few businesses off of them and done okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Now, one of the things that I have learned through the last 10, 15 years of doing this is that when you develop a side project and you turn it into a business and you ship it, you're actually only at, you're solving 30% of the problem.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You may be wondering, what do I mean?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: This comes from a saying that my friend Rob Walling from the Startups for the Rest of Us podcast has.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: He says, shipping your software product is only 30% of the problem.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: The other 70% is sales and marketing.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So that's where this book, Great Leads, comes into play.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's a book on how to write the good lead into your copy.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And your copy is something that's going to help sell your software if you're writing software like I am.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I'm writing a SaaS app, and I want to make sure that
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: when someone lands on my homepage, they understand immediately what it's about and it hooks them to continue reading and learning about the product.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Again, maybe I've already shipped it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's 30% of the problem.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So this kind of really hooks back into this podcast here by...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: helping you understand what technology you want to learn next.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And this applies to not just technology, but just any subject that you want to learn next.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But I'll keep it focused on technology today.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But I did want to share that even if you are heavily laden into technology like I am, it's good to step out of your area of comfort and focus on other things that are complementary skills.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And in my opinion, copywriting is one of those.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And that's what I'm doing here on the side is reading this copywriting book.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But we're not going to dive into that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: We'll talk about that another time.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's just kind of related.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So how do you pick which technology to learn next?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: This is a very difficult one, and it's one that I didn't really have a good standard practice for until I sat down and started hammering out.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah, yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: and also learned other skills along the way, such as Node.js.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And I've learned some Go.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I've learned TypeScript recently.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I've learned React.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I've learned Rails, Python, you name it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I've learned a bunch of different stuff, Docker, Kubernetes I'm working on now.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so a lot of times people say, well, there's all this stuff out there.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: How do you decide what tech to work on?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So let's just go ahead and hop into the list that I've created here.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And the first item here is to understand that you can't learn it all.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: There's just no way.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: The amount of technical content out there is astronomical.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I mean, it's huge.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You probably know that just from being in the industry.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And this applies also as if you're just getting out of college.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's overwhelming to think about, do I want to be a mobile developer?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Do I want to be a network engineer?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Do I want to be a C developer that goes and works in video games?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: What do I want to do?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Or maybe you're like me and you have interests everywhere.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I look at my wireless printer over here on the desk next to me and I think, I bet you I could program that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And I have to reel myself back in to realize that I can't learn at all.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Because there's no way, there's not enough time in the day.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So I have to figure out a way to come up with a framework to filter out what I can learn and should learn.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So that leads us to the number two item here.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And that is identifying if it's a hobby versus a value skill.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Is it a hobby skill or a value skill?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So what does that mean?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Let's say that you're interested in
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: something like Arduino and you are just, you want to experiment with it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You want to tinker with it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You want to make something that can automatically lock your doors or turn on certain lights with sensors.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And you just find a lot of interest and it's a really fun thing to do that you like to do during your free time.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's a hobby.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If you have the time and a free time, then I advise you just go do it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's something that's going to provide you value just on your own personal psychological level.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: there's nothing wrong with chasing those hobbies, as long as it's not impeding upon your regular day-to-day work, of course.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So if it's a hobby, yeah, go for it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If it's a value skill, well, then we still have some more filtering that we need to do.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So if that's gonna be, maybe you realize, well, hey, I'm kind of interested in building maybe some, a web API, but I'm not sure if I should use Rust or Go or Node.js or Rails.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I don't know which one to do.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Well, at that point, it's, you kind of have to evaluate
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Number three here, which is, is this new technology or am I gonna do some deep dive into an existing technology?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And what I mean by this is let's assume that you're an Android developer.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And like I said, maybe you have interest in building a web API.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So that's gonna be a new technology for you.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So that would be the new tech side.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So you have to decide is this new tech or a deep dive into existing tech.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And this is a distinction where you want to move as a developer.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: When you are going to be deep diving into tech, you may decide to say, I'm going to stay inside of the Android ecosystem.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I can already build apps in it, but now I really want to dive deep into the Bluetooth stack because I like that Bluetooth stack.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So let's decide you do that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You can dive into that deep dive of that stack and learn everything that there is to learn about...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Bluetooth and all the things around it, Bluetooth, low energy, devices, connections, audio, you name it, you're going to learn everything.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: There's a couple of things that are going to happen here.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: This is going to make you a specialist.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Now, a specialist inside of Bluetooth of Android, you could command a pretty good dollar for the right company.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: They may need a expert who is a, you know, someone who knows Bluetooth in and out and you might be that person and you can charge a pretty penny to work as that Bluetooth expert.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: However,
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It also has a flip side.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: When you deep dive into existing technology and you pigeonhole yourself into one technology, that can also be seen as very fragile.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Now let's assume that you are the Bluetooth person like we're talking about.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And for whatever reason, next week, a brand new wireless communication technology comes out that just deprecates everything that's Bluetooth related.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It just makes Bluetooth looks like child's play.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: This can happen.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It probably will happen one day.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: At that point in time, if your entire career is vested into Bluetooth, you now have a humongous risk area that you need to address of, well, what does my future look like?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Because now it looks like Bluetooth is not going to be the thing anymore.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Great.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: What do I do now?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So again, it can be a good thing or it can be a bad thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It depends on what you really want to dive deep into.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And no technology is immune from being around forever.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: There's always going to be legacy code of some sort, but things are always going to change inside of your technology.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I'm not saying you should choose one or the other because if you go into new tech and you choose the wrong technology, you could be in the same place.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Wow, I learned to work with this brand new web communication technology that no one ended up using.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And this happens a lot, especially when companies like Facebook or Google release a new technology and it looks super great and you try it and then you realize that no one really catches on to it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So both those things can happen.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So you do need to decide which area you're going to go, new tech or deep dive into existing.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Either one is fine.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That brings us to the next item.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Number four, you have to be interested in whatever you decide you're going to be doing.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If it's going to be building a web API and you have to be interested in doing it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If you have no interest in building web APIs, you are not going to learn it that well.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You're going to literally be fighting an uphill battle the entire time.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If you want to learn, if you're an Android developer and you want to learn how to make games and that's all you can eat and drink and think about is games.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Well, it might be a good time to be a game developer in the Android ecosystem.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So that is a very good time for you to look at something like that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: However, if again, if you do not find any interest in it, you're gonna find yourself being very bored.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You're not gonna wanna do it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And eventually you're just gonna burn out on it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's not gonna be something you wanna do.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So number four, this is probably one of the most important actually, is do you have any interest in it?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If you wanna learn to build a web API, but you absolutely hate JavaScript, I mean, you despise it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: well then learning Node.js is probably not gonna be that enjoyable for you.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You might have a better time learning something with Ktor if you're a Kotlin person or using Rails API if you don't mind Ruby or Python or any of the other languages out there.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Maybe Node.js just isn't your thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You have to find interest in it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Which brings us to number five.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: is this a comparable skill or this brand new skill set that you can't even apply to anything that you're doing?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And what does that mean?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: A comparable skill is gonna be, again, assuming you're an Android developer, a comparable skill would be, I know how to develop an API inside of Rails or Node.js or whatever, but I can develop an API.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's a comparable skill because now I can develop the backend technology using, it could be Kotlin, JavaScript, Ruby, whatever,
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I can also develop the front end, which is going to be my Android app.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's a very comparable skill.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: This can be very valuable going forward, especially for clients or a company you're working for, because now maybe you've finished all the features on the Android app, but the backend needs some work.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If you know how to do that kind of work, you're much more valuable overall.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Now that shouldn't deter you from learning a new skillset.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So again, let's say that you're an Android developer.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You have no interest in
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: and learning how to build a web backend.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But maybe you have interest in building iOS apps.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's another skillset you could hop into.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So maybe you have the interest in it and you want to build iOS apps.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's fine.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You can start learning iOS apps.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's going to give you different skillset, but it's going to be also somewhat comparable because it's also in the mobile space.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Now, what would be a completely different skill set of something that is completely different?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Maybe something like game programming for consoles.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's completely different.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Maybe programming C-level stereos or assembly for your car radio or something like that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That might be something completely different.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Programming circuits.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Again, IoT.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Completely different as long as you're in a different environment.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Those are going to be different skill sets.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So none of these are any, all these are not going to be any wrong paths for you to go down.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You just have to identify where you find the interest.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Now, once you've kind of decided, all right, well, I know that I want to start working with, let's say APIs again.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Once you decide that you want to start building something and I want to build this in Node.js.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: you need to go build something with it and actually ship it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Now, what I usually recommend folks do if they don't have an app that they're working on is to just build a note-taking app.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And you're gonna build the same note-taking app in tons of different languages throughout your career.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That note-taking app should have reminders and dates on it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: This is simply because anytime you introduce all these things, you're gonna have to worry about storage, you're gonna have to worry about inputs, you're gonna have to worry about validation, you're gonna have to worry about dates, date math, some types of reminders.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Anything, you know, maybe push notifications if you're working with mobile.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: There's a ton of things that are involved in a simple note-taking app, even authentication and authorization of who can, if it's a team-based app, who can do these different things.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: There's quite a bit involved in a very simple note-taking app.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Even if it's just for you, just build something and ship it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Just go do it.

text: If you're looking for something to learn as a hobby, just go do it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's fun and it should be fun.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Don't overthink it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Number three, is this new technology or am I going to deep dive into an existing technology that I already know?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So you need to decide which area am I going to go into.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then once you do that, you have to ask yourself, am I going to be interested in this new thing?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If I'm going to be an Android person and do the deep dive into Bluetooth, am I really interested in Bluetooth?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Or am I just doing it because my job has deep Bluetooth stuff and it's what I need to learn?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Okay, maybe you need to learn it for your job.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: There's no problem with that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But is it something you really want to spend your free time on learning?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Maybe not.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Maybe you want to learn that new tech.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Maybe you want to learn Kotlin backend.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Maybe you want to learn how to build a site in Django with Python.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So those are good options.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So you've got to be interested in it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's the key.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's really the key here.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's right in the middle, but it really is the key to everything.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You have to be interested in it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Number five, is it a comparable skill or brand new skill set?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Is this a skill that I could use on a day-to-day basis, perhaps at another client, perhaps at my job?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If I'm a mobile developer, learning how to program an API is going to help me.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's going to expand my skill set.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's a comparable skill.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Or is it a brand new skill set?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If I have never really worked with Postgres databases and I wanna learn that from the ground up, that's a brand new skillset.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Still valuable, you just have to realize where it fits into your repertoire of skills.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And finally, number six, once you decide what you wanna work with, doesn't matter what it is, you need to go build something with it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Actually build something and actually ship it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: This is going to be open your eyes up to if you really like it or not.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Now there's a caveat I should have mentioned before.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: When you're in the middle of building this, if you get halfway in and you realize this is absolutely miserable, I hate this, stop.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: There's so much technology for you to learn out there that you just need to stop and go chase something different.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Now, if you're continually having this problem over and over and over, maybe you're hitting a roadblock.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You need to push through that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's understandable.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But if you realize that you're getting into building something and you realize, I really don't like this, it's time to stop and reevaluate if this is something that you really want to work on or not.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And if not, go back and pick something different.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's my method and my framework for how I pick what technology to learn next.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And really it's heavily weighted on, does this interest me?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And is this a marketable skill that I can use going forward?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If it doesn't interest me, most likely I probably won't learn it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And the only time that's outweighed is if it's going to be a marketable skill that I can use going forward.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: For example, years prior, you couldn't have paid me to read a copywriting book.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: However, I have recently found over the last couple of years that I actually kind of enjoy learning about that process, about how people think about how products, about the products that they want to use.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That helps me develop products better on a software perspective because I can think about these things in advance.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So again, I wouldn't just pigeonhole yourself to technology in certain regards, even though that's what this podcast is about.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You can think about this framework in a one number of other ways as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I hope that helps.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Hope you find a technology that you're interested in learning next.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: As for me, if I were to say what technology should you learn next?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I'm not going to give you that answer, actually.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's something you have to go out and explore on your own.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Make sure you're interested in it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And if you can, send me a message on Twitter and let me know what you choose to work on next.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I'm interested in hearing what you folks are working on.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Thanks for listening, and we'll catch you in the next episode.
